titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Josephine and Antonio Bouzas Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Robert Bartels

conventiondeclaration.abbr: Finding aid created in accordance with Manuscripts and Archives Processing Manual
conventiondeclaration.citation: translation missing: en.enumerations.resource_finding_aid_description_rules.Finding aid created in accordance with Manuscripts and Archives Processing Manual

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday January 23, 2020 at 08:20

did.abstract: The Josephine and Antonio Bouzas papers contain photographs, legal documents, and correspondence documenting the history of the Josephine and Antonio Bouzas family and their experiences during the Spanish Civil War.

langmaterial.language: The materials are in Spanish and English.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The collection is open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Josephine and Antonio Bouzas Papers (MS 2112). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Josephine Bouzas (née Varela) was born on November 18, 1913, in Manhattan, New York. Her mother and father, Carmen Varela Garcia and Jesus Varela Lendoiro, were Spanish immigrants. Jesephine's parents owned and operated a boarding house for sailors and a billiard parlor and tavern. At a young age Josephine and her mother emigrated back to Spain; her father, having prospered in the United States, joined them in the late 1920s. They lived in the Galicia region in Northern Spain. In the 1930s, the family witnessed the civil unrest and increasing violence developing in Spain, culminating in the start of the Spanish Civil War in 1936. Josephine's father was arrested and beaten by fascist paramilitary forces. Josephine, her father, and her brother Pepe had United States passports and were therefore able to flee to the United States, where they were taken in by relatives.

Antonio Bouzas was born on April 26, 1917 in Esteiro, a coastal village in Northern Spain. At age eighteen, Antonio joined the Navy. When the Spanish Civil War broke out, Antonio fought for the Nationalists. After the Civil War, Antonio worked on fishing trawlers and cargo ships. Antonio and Josephine met in 1950 at a dance in Manhattan, New York. The couple were married and had two daughters. The family lived on West 65th Street in Manhattan and ran a local grocery store.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Josephine and Antonio Bouzas papers document the history of the Josephine and Antonio Bouzas family and their experiences during the Spanish Civil War. The collection includes photographs taken in Northern Spain in the 1930s before the start of the Spanish Civil War, as well as photographs of once the family emigrated to Manhattan, New York, in the 1940s. The collection also consists of correspondence and legal documents relating to life in Spain during the time of the Spanish Civil War, as well as an essay by Josephine and Antonio's daughter Marta on her family history and life as an immigrant in The United States. The collection also includes a scarf, made in 1939 as a souvenir for a dance given by the "Circulo Valenciano de New York" in honor of those who died fighting for the Republic, and Antonio Bouzas's maritime registration book, which documents his time in the Spanish Navy.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright is retained by Marta Barbeosch Varela for materials she has authored or otherwise produced. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

geogname.part: Spain -- History Civil War, 1936-1939 -- Personal narratives, Spanish

did.unittitle: "Between the steely hands of the proletariat, fascism will die!": Reflections on my personal legacy of the Spanish Civil War by Marta Barbeosch Varela

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs are of Josephine Bouzas as a child in Manhattan, New York, and of Josephine and her family in Spain before the Spanish Civil War.

did.unittitle: "Mis Fotos" [My Photos], album of postcards of La Coruña

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains photographs of Bouzas family in both Spain and Manhattan, New York, as well as photographs of Josephine and Antonio's daughter Marta.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: On the corners of the scarf are images of Spanish Republic General José Miaja, members of the Republican militia, and a guardian lion. In the center is an image of a bloody fist squeezing an enemy officer. Enscribed are the words "Entre Los Dedos Acerados Del Proletariado Morira El Fascismo" ("Between the steely fingers of the proletariat Fascism will die").

did.unittitle: United States Department of State, correspondence and legal documents
